title: Supporting the designation of April 10 as "Dolores Huerta Day", in honor of the accomplishments and legacy of the trailblazing labor and civil rights leader Dolores Huerta.
plain_summary: This bill supports making April 10th a special day called "Dolores Huerta Day" to honor an important civil rights leader. Dolores Huerta spent her life fighting for farm workers' rights and women's equality. She helped start labor unions and organized boycotts to help workers get fair treatment. The bill recognizes all the awards she has won and the work she continues to do through her foundation to fight for equal rights for all people.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution supports making April tenth a special day called Dolores Huerta Day. This day would honor the work and achievements of Dolores Huerta, who was a groundbreaking leader in the fight for workers' rights and civil rights.

sections.title: Background on Dolores Huerta
sections.text: Dolores Huerta was born on April tenth, nineteen thirty, in Dawson, New Mexico. She started fighting for people's rights in nineteen fifty-five when she helped create a chapter of an organization in Stockton, California. This group worked to help Latino people vote and improve their economic conditions. In nineteen sixty-two, she worked with Cesar Chavez to start the National Farm Workers Association, which later became the United Farm Workers Union. This union helped protect farm workers and fight for their rights.

sections.title: Major Accomplishments
sections.text: Dolores Huerta was the main person behind nationwide boycotts of table grapes in the late nineteen sixties. These boycotts meant people refused to buy grapes to pressure grape companies to treat workers better. By nineteen seventy, this led to a successful union contract. She helped organize a strike of five thousand grape workers in nineteen sixty-five in Delano and was the main person who negotiated the contract that came from it. She led another grape boycott that helped pass the California Agricultural Labor Relations Act of nineteen seventy-five. This was the first law in the United States that allowed farm workers to join together and bargain as a group.

sections.title: Advocacy Work
sections.text: Dolores Huerta worked hard to make working conditions safer for farm workers, including getting rid of harmful pesticides (chemicals used to kill bugs that can hurt people too). She spoke up for women's rights and equality within the farm workers movement. She created the phrase Si Se Puede, which means Yes We Can in Spanish. This became a famous saying for the farm workers movement and for fighting injustice everywhere. Even when police and farm owners used violence against her, she stayed committed to peaceful protest and civil rights activism.

sections.title: Recognition and Honors
sections.text: In nineteen ninety-three, Dolores Huerta became the first Latina woman inducted into the National Women's Hall of Fame. She worked later in her career to help more Latino people and women get elected to political office. She traveled across the country for two years as part of a campaign to encourage Latina women to run for office. This resulted in many more women being elected at local, state, and federal levels. She also served as National Chair of the Twenty-first Century Party, which was founded in nineteen ninety-two with the goal that women would make up fifty-two percent of candidates and that officers would represent the ethnic diversity of the nation.

sections.title: Awards and Continuing Work
sections.text: Dolores Huerta has received many awards, including the Eleanor Roosevelt Human Rights Award in nineteen ninety-eight and the Presidential Medal of Freedom in twenty twelve. The Presidential Medal of Freedom is the highest award a civilian can receive. She holds nine honorary doctorates from universities including Princeton University, Mills College, the University of the Pacific, and the University of Southern California. In nineteen ninety-eight, Ladies Home Journal named her one of the one hundred Most Important Women of the Twentieth Century. In twenty zero three, she founded the Dolores Huerta Foundation to continue her work fighting for equality and civil rights. She currently serves as president of this foundation.

sections.title: Resolution Statement
sections.text: The House of Representatives supports making Dolores Huerta Day an official designation and honors Dolores Huerta's legacy and contributions to advancing civil rights and equality. The United States should continue to advance the efforts that Dolores Huerta started to ensure equal protection under the law and civil rights for all people in the United States.
